main_summary.summary: Maynard E. Moore was interviewed by David Cline of Virginia Tech, working with the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, on December 14, 2015, on the campus of Wesley Theological Seminary. The recorded segment is brief and covers only the opening biographical material. Moore was born in Petersburg, Virginia, in 1938 and raised in a Southern town with a population of about 35,000, a Civil War site (the Crater) and a tobacco railhead whose economy was reinforced by the Army's Camp Lee, later Fort Lee. His family attended Washington Street United Methodist Church, one of ten Methodist churches in the segregated town. His home church carries a placard marking the 1846 organization of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, formed in the schism over slavery. Moore felt the call to ministry there, participated in the Methodist Student Movement at the district level, and was elected to a state-wide Methodist Student Movement office as a high school senior. He describes a childhood of playing with Black children in Petersburg until junior high, when his mother explained that those friendships would no longer be permitted at home.
key_themes: Methodist Episcopal Church, South and the 1846 slavery schism
main_summary.historical_significance:
Moore's testimony documents the lived texture of segregated white Southern Methodist childhood in mid-twentieth-century Petersburg, Virginia, including the specific moment at which cross-racial childhood friendships were severed by family rule at the threshold of junior high school. It also locates the Washington Street United Methodist Church as the 1846 organizing site of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, anchoring a denominational schism over slavery in a specific building still standing during Moore's youth.

As a primary source the interview pairs this segregated-childhood narrative with Moore's entry into the Methodist Student Movement at the district and state-wide levels during high school, marking the youth-ministry pathway by which some white Southern Methodists later moved into Civil Rights-era ministry. The recorded segment is short and ends mid-thought; later portions of Moore's life and movement work are not captured here.

chapters.title: Interview Setup and Introductions
chapters.summary: Interviewer David Cline of Virginia Tech, working with the Southern Oral History Program at UNC Chapel Hill, opens the December 14, 2015 session at Wesley Theological Seminary. Moore identifies himself and notes he has lived in Washington for thirty-five years.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording session, December 14, 2015

chapters.title: Born in Petersburg, Virginia
chapters.summary: Moore states he was born in Petersburg, Virginia, in 1938 and was raised there. He frames Petersburg as a Southern town in important respects and signals that being born and raised there is essential context for understanding him.

chapters.title: Petersburg as Civil War and Tobacco Town
chapters.summary: Moore describes Petersburg's local history: the Crater, where Union troops tunneled under Confederate works and detonated dynamite by accidental trigger, now a historic site that townspeople treated as a prominent event. He also describes Petersburg as a long-standing commercial center, a railhead and switching area for tobacco, with the early-twentieth-century establishment of Camp Lee, later Fort Lee, as an Army quartermaster depot that boosted the economy through World War II.
related_events: Establishment of Camp Lee / Fort Lee

chapters.title: Methodist Family and the 1846 Schism
chapters.summary: Moore describes his Methodist upbringing at Washington Street United Methodist Church, one of ten Methodist churches in a town of 35,000 with an equivalent number of Baptist and other churches, all segregated. He notes the placard on his home church marking the 1846 organization of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, formed when Southern Methodists broke from the northern Methodist church over slavery.
related_events: 1846 organization of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South

chapters.title: Call to Ministry and Methodist Student Movement
chapters.summary: Moore identifies Washington Street United Methodist Church as the place he felt the call to ministry. He recounts his participation in the youth group and the Methodist Student Movement at the district level, and his election to a state-wide Methodist Student Movement office as a senior in high school.

chapters.title: Segregation Unspoken at Home and Church
chapters.summary: Asked whether segregation or racial issues were discussed at home or in the church, Moore says nobody addressed racial issues, probably by unspoken design. He describes himself as asleep on these matters during those years; it was simply the way of life and not on his family's radar.

chapters.title: Petersburg's Cross-Street Geography of Race
chapters.summary: Moore describes the spatial organization of Petersburg, where his family lived on a main cross street. As was typical of Southern towns, the cross streets running off the main streets were where the Black population lived, less cared for than the main streets. Black neighbors were nearby but not on the main thoroughfares.

chapters.title: End of Cross-Racial Childhood Friendships
chapters.summary: Moore recounts playing with Black children until junior high, when his mother explained that they would no longer be welcome in the house as had previously been the case. He had invited Black friends in to watch the Howdy Doody show or share cookies and ice cream and had visited their houses; from junior high on, there was no more interaction, though some had been among his best buddies. The recorded segment ends here.
